--- title: "Global Cybersecurity Threats" subtitle: "2015–2024 · Incident Patterns Across Countries, Industries & Attack Vectors" author: "Vithujan Shanmugaratnam · Francis Lehner · Brian Bernhauser" date: "ZHAW — VDSS Project 2026" format: revealjs: theme: dark transition: fade transition-speed: fast slide-number: c/t footer: "VDSS | Global Cybersecurity Threat Analysis 2015–2024" fig-align: center embed-resources: true progress: true code-fold: true highlight-style: github-dark width: 1280 height: 720 execute: echo: false warning: false message: false --- ```{=html} ``` --- ## Agenda
1
**Problem & Solution** — Why this project?
2
**Audience** — Who are our users?
3
**Data Source** — Dataset overview & acquisition
4
**Exploratory Analysis** — What the data reveals
5
**Visualisation Product** — Design decisions & live examples
6
**Key Findings & Summary**
::: notes Welcome everyone. We'll move through six sections today, each building on the previous one. The last section shows actual charts from the Quarto site we built. ::: # 1 · Problem & Solution {.center} ## The Problem **Cybersecurity teams drown in raw data — not insight.** . . . - Incident tables grow to thousands of rows per reporting cycle - Comparing *frequency* vs. *severity* requires manual aggregation - Cross-dimensional patterns (country × industry × attack type) stay hidden - Every reporting cycle: export CSV → build charts in Excel → assemble slides → repeat . . . ::: {.callout-warning appearance="minimal"} **Today's reality:** Analysts spend hours on mechanics, not analysis. ::: ::: notes The core tension: data is abundant, insight is scarce. The manual workflow is not only slow — it introduces errors every cycle. ::: ## What We Built An **interactive, reproducible web report** turning 3,000 raw incidents into navigable insight: :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="50%"} **Five analytical sections:** - Headline KPIs & scale-setting - Temporal trends (2015–2024) - Country & industry comparisons - Attack type & vulnerability profiles - Defense mechanism outcomes ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} **Technical stack:** - Single **Quarto** document → HTML report - Python (pandas · matplotlib · seaborn) - Published on **GitHub Pages** — no install required - Fully reproducible from a fresh session ::: :::: ::: notes One Quarto source file renders the entire report. Any team member can re-run it after a new data export and the site updates automatically. ::: # 2 · Audience {.center} ## Persona 1 — Sophia, Senior Cybersecurity Analyst :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="58%"} **Profile** - Age 34 · Security Operations Centre - Prepares trend analysis and internal reports regularly - **High** domain expertise and data literacy - 27″ desktop monitor — happy to explore deeply
"I need to see which vulnerability types are most exploited in Banking — and whether that pattern differs from Healthcare."
::: ::: {.column width="42%"} **Jobs to be done** - Analyse attack trends by year, country, industry - Compare vulnerabilities and defense outcomes - Produce evidence-based briefings **Pain points** - Combining static charts is time-consuming - Existing summaries lack multi-dimensional comparison - Hard to separate frequent threats from severe ones ::: :::: ## Persona 2 — Marcus, IT Security Manager :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="58%"} **Profile** - Age 48 · Responsible for security priorities at management level - Communicates risk to senior leadership - **Medium** data literacy — limited time for exploration - 14″ corporate laptop — needs fast time-to-insight
"Show me the most affected industries and the biggest financial losses. I need to justify our budget priorities."
::: ::: {.column width="42%"} **Jobs to be done** - Quick overview of key threat patterns - Identify high-risk sectors and attack types - Communicate findings to executive stakeholders **Pain points** - Complex dashboards create friction and reduce trust - Technical views don't translate to management communication - Can't distinguish frequency from severity at a glance ::: :::: ::: notes Marcus and Sophia pull in opposite directions on depth vs. speed. The report resolves this tension through a layered design: overview on load, detail on drill-down. ::: # 3 · Data Source {.center} ## Kaggle — Global Cybersecurity Threats (2015–2024) :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="55%"} **What is it?** A public dataset of **3,000 cybersecurity incidents** spanning 2015–2024, published on Kaggle under a CC0 licence. **Scope** - **10 countries:** AUS · BRA · CHN · FRA · DEU · IND · JPN · RUS · GBR · USA - **7 industries:** Banking · Education · Government · Healthcare · IT · Retail · Telecom - **10 variables** per incident — no missing values, no imputation required ::: ::: {.column width="45%"} | Variable | Role | |---|---| | Country, Year | Context | | Attack Type, Attack Source | Threat | | Target Industry, Vulnerability | Target | | Defense Mechanism | Response | | Financial Loss (M$) | Severity | | Affected Users | Severity | | Resolution Time (h) | Severity | ::: :::: ::: notes The dataset is synthetic and evenly balanced — no country or industry dominates raw counts. This means cross-category comparisons are meaningful, and we can describe patterns without worrying about sampling bias. ::: ## Data Acquisition & Pipeline **Automated download via `data_acquisition/load.py`:** ```python import kaggle kaggle.api.dataset_download_files( "atharvasoundankar/global-cybersecurity-threats-2015-2024", path="data/raw/", unzip=True ) ``` - API credentials stored in `.env` — never committed to version control - Column names normalised to `lowercase_snake_case` at load time - Any team member can reproduce acquisition independently - Zero missing values — no imputation or exclusion needed # 4 · Exploratory Analysis {.center} ## At a Glance — Key Numbers ::: {#cell-fig-kpis .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Headline metrics across all 3,000 incidents.](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-kpis-output-1.png){#fig-kpis width=1542 height=320} ::: ::: ::: notes The numbers are not surprising in isolation, but they set scale. $49 M average loss and 49-hour average resolution time are the baselines our visualisations measure everything against. ::: ## Balanced Coverage — Country & Industry ::: {#cell-fig-eda-overview .cell execution_count=3} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Incident counts by country (left) and industry (right). All categories are evenly distributed.](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-eda-overview-output-1.png){#fig-eda-overview width=1521 height=509} ::: ::: All categories are **evenly distributed** — the dataset is synthetic and balanced, making cross-category comparisons meaningful without sampling corrections. ## Three Independent Dimensions of Severity ::: {#cell-fig-eda-corr .cell execution_count=4} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Pearson correlations between the three numerical severity measures.](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-eda-corr-output-1.png){#fig-eda-corr width=672 height=573} ::: ::: Near-zero correlations confirm that **financial loss, affected users, and resolution time are independent** — each adds unique information and all three must be surfaced in the report. ## Attack Types & Vulnerability Distribution ::: {#cell-fig-eda-attack .cell execution_count=5} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Frequency of attack types (left) and vulnerability types (right).](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-eda-attack-output-1.png){#fig-eda-attack width=1523 height=509} ::: ::: # 5 · Visualisation Product {.center} ## Product Structure — From Overview to Detail The report is divided into **five sections** supporting both quick orientation and deeper analysis: | Section | Primary Content | User | |---|---|---| | **Overview** | KPI cards, headline totals, sparkline | Marcus | | **Trends** | Incidents & severity over 2015–2024 | Both | | **Countries & Industries** | Frequency and severity rankings | Both | | **Threat Profiles** | Attack types, vulnerabilities, attack sources | Sophia | | **Defense & Outcomes** | Mechanisms vs. resolution time & loss | Sophia | ::: notes The overview-to-detail principle means Marcus gets his answer on page load, while Sophia can keep scrolling. No user has to wade through complexity they don't need. ::: ## Overview — Setting Scale Immediately :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="44%"} **Design rationale** - KPI cards answer "how big is the problem?" before any filtering - Familiar management communication format for Marcus - Sparkline gives temporal context without needing a full trend chart **What's surfaced** - Total incident count - Average & total financial loss - Average resolution time - Most frequent attack type ::: ::: {.column width="56%"} ::: {#cell-fig-prod-kpi .cell execution_count=6} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-prod-kpi-output-1.png){#fig-prod-kpi width=797 height=485} ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Trends — How Threats Evolved Over a Decade :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="44%"} **Why line charts?** - Time-series data maps naturally to connected lines - Filled area emphasises cumulative scale - Metric toggle (count / loss / users / time) avoids chart overload while keeping all severity dimensions accessible **Design choice:** keep one metric visible at a time to prevent cognitive overload ::: ::: {.column width="56%"} ::: {#cell-fig-prod-trend .cell execution_count=7} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-prod-trend-output-1.png){#fig-prod-trend width=825 height=501} ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Countries & Industries — Ranking What Matters :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="44%"} **Why sorted bars — not a map?** - Dataset contains only 10 countries → precise ranking outweighs geography - Sorted bars make order unambiguous at a glance - A choropleth would add visual complexity without analytical gain **Metric toggle:** count / loss / users / resolution time lets Marcus and Sophia pull the same chart for different answers ::: ::: {.column width="56%"} ::: {#cell-fig-prod-industry .cell execution_count=8} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-prod-industry-output-1.png){#fig-prod-industry width=829 height=501} ::: ::: ::: :::: ::: notes Sophia uses this chart by industry. Marcus uses it to justify budget allocation toward the hardest-hit sectors. ::: ## Threat Profiles — Concentration at a Glance :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="44%"} **Why heatmaps?** - Encode two categorical dimensions (attack × industry) without requiring separate charts per combination - Colour intensity immediately highlights concentration - Answers Sophia's question: *"Which vulnerability types are most common in Banking?"* **No causal claims** — dataset is synthetic; patterns are described as within-data observations ::: ::: {.column width="56%"} ::: {#cell-fig-prod-heatmap .cell execution_count=9} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-prod-heatmap-output-1.png){#fig-prod-heatmap width=847 height=525} ::: ::: ::: :::: ## Defense & Outcomes — Do Defenses Make a Difference? :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="44%"} **Why box plots?** - Distributions reveal spread and outliers — not just averages - Avoids misleading summary statistics when underlying data is wide - Sorted by median → ranking is immediately visible **Key question for Sophia:** Are mechanisms like AI-based Detection or Encryption genuinely associated with lower losses — or is it noise? ::: ::: {.column width="56%"} ::: {#cell-fig-prod-boxplot .cell execution_count=10} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-prod-boxplot-output-1.png){#fig-prod-boxplot width=850 height=501} ::: ::: ::: :::: ::: notes The overlapping distributions suggest the dataset's synthetic nature — real-world data would likely show sharper separation. We annotate this caveat directly in the report. ::: ## Design Decisions — Summary | Decision | Rationale | |---|---| | **Sorted bar charts** over choropleth maps | Only 10 countries — ranking beats geography | | **Metric toggle** (count / loss / users / time) | Explicit frequency vs. severity distinction; avoids chart overload | | **Box plots** for defense outcomes | Distributions are more honest than means for skewed severity data | | **Heatmaps** for cross-category analysis | Compact, two-dimensional categorical relationships | | **Overview → detail** structure | Fast entry for Marcus; depth for Sophia | | **No causal claims** in annotations | Dataset is synthetic — findings described as within-data patterns | | **GitHub Pages deployment** | Reproducible, version-controlled, no installation required | # 6 · Key Findings & Summary {.center} ## What the Data Tells Us ::: {#cell-fig-summary .cell execution_count=11} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Top: incident trend over time. Bottom-left: loss by industry. Bottom-right: defense outcome distributions.](presentation_improved_files/figure-revealjs/fig-summary-output-1.png){#fig-summary width=1413 height=668} ::: ::: ::: notes The three panels together tell the story: loss is stable year-on-year (no clear escalation), varies moderately by industry, and distributions overlap heavily across defense types — a finding worth investigating with richer, real-world data. ::: ## Summary & Next Steps :::: {.columns} ::: {.column width="55%"} **What we delivered** - A **single-source, reproducible** Quarto report rendering to GitHub Pages - Five analytical sections covering trend, geography, industry, threat, and defense - Dual-persona design: fast overview for Marcus, depth for Sophia - All charts use consistent, accessible colour coding and honest distribution displays **Key data findings** - Severity dimensions (loss, users, time) are statistically independent - All countries and industries share similar incident exposure - Defense mechanism outcomes overlap widely in this synthetic dataset ::: ::: {.column width="45%"} **If extended with real data:** - Replace balanced synthetic records with live SIEM exports - Add interactive filters (Plotly / Observable) for sector-specific drilldown - Introduce time-lagged correlations between attack volume and financial impact - Automate periodic refresh via GitHub Actions ::: {.callout-note appearance="minimal"} **Reproducibility:** The entire report renders from a fresh Python environment with a single `quarto render` command. ::: ::: :::: --- ## {.center}
**Global Cybersecurity Threats 2015–2024** *A Visual Data Story for Security Teams*
Vithujan Shanmugaratnam · Francis Lehner · Brian Bernhauser **ZHAW — VDSS Project 2026**
::: {style="font-size:0.8em; opacity:0.55;"} Data: Kaggle CC0 · Built with Quarto + Python · Source on GitHub :::
